response:
I read both files and weighed the two probes. Decision recorded at `docs/decisions/0045-sync-check-script-not-claim-protocol.md` (5,826/6,000 chars).

## The call

**Prose alone is not enough — this repo already proved that.** ADR 0042 shows a *settled, superseded* prose rule (fraction-met) still floored a third time with nothing mechanical catching it (docs/decisions/0042-...md:13,22). ISSUE-106.md's own Gap section admits the sync rule as drafted has the identical weakness (ISSUE-106.md:14). The premortem's finding #1 is exactly this precedent — I couldn't defend shipping the rule as pure CLAUDE.md prose.

**But full lock-based mechanization isn't achievable now either.** This repo's only forcing functions (`adr-lint`, muda-review CI) gate at PR-review time — after spend already happened — and the harness dispatching `claude -p` sessions is external to this repo, so there's no pre-spend hook point to attach a real block to. That's the assumption probe's option-4 falsifier, and it holds.

**So: mechanize what's actually checkable, reject what's already broken.** Ship the sync check as a runnable, read-only script (`scripts/check-issue-live.mjs`) — fetch + issue-state + referencing-PR search, binary STALE/PROCEED, run before execution starts and again before final push — instead of a paragraph. Reject the claim protocol outright, not defer it: its premise (issue-write permission at dispatch) is falsified in the issue's own text (ISSUE-106.md:15), and per the premortem's finding #2, a claim signal that silently no-ops for some sessions is *worse* than no signal — false confidence, not a weaker safeguard.

## Load-bearing assumption + accepted failure class

**Assumption:** a runnable script with a binary verdict gets invoked more reliably under dispatch pressure than the equivalent prose paragraph — even though invocation is still voluntary, since no harness hook forces the call.
If false, this is no better than the prose rule it replaces, and duplicate-spend recurs exactly as in the original incident.

**Accepted failure class:** concurrent-instant dispatch collisions (premortem finding #3) — two sessions checking within the same window, both seeing genuinely-open state — stay explicitly open. Closing that needs an atomic lock this repo's stack can't guarantee (no controllable pre-spend hook, and claim-based locking is precisely the mechanism just rejected for its permission hole). This decision closes the *sequential* staleness bug that actually occurred (fresh-at-start, stale-by-use); it does not claim to close true concurrency.

One caveat: Bash in this session required approval for every `node` invocation, so I couldn't run `adr-lint` myself — please run `node pdca-workflow/scripts/adr-lint.mjs docs/decisions` before merging, per the repo's own sacred rule.

title: "Duplicate-spend: script-backed sync check ships; claim protocol rejected"
status: accepted
summary: "Mechanize sync-before-spend as a runnable read-only script (git fetch + issue-state + referencing-PR search, binary STALE/PROCEED) run at execution-start and before final push, not left as CLAUDE.md prose. Reject the claim protocol outright — its issue-write-at-dispatch premise is already falsified in ISSUE-106.md:15. Concurrent-instant dispatch collisions stay explicitly open — no lock closes them."
---

# 0045 — Duplicate-spend: script-backed sync check ships; claim protocol rejected

- Date: 2026-07-14
- Owner: PM
- Panel: lean-process-engineer, session-operator, process-economist.
- Context: #30 executed twice in parallel — a session's clone was fresh-at-start but stale-by-use over a long run, caught only by luck at end-of-session `git fetch` (ISSUE-106.md:6). Nothing forces a re-sync/re-read before spend or a visible claim (ISSUE-106.md:8-10). The issue's own Gap section already flags the sync rule as "prose-only (same enforcement weakness as any prose rule)" (ISSUE-106.md:14), and this repo has a live precedent for that exact weakness: ADR 0025's fraction-met metric, a settled prose supersession, still floored a third time because nothing mechanically enforced it (docs/decisions/0042-experiment-methodology-and-prespend-discipline.md:13,22).

## Decision
Ship the sync check as a runnable script, not a paragraph: `scripts/check-issue-live.mjs <issue-number>` does `git fetch origin main`, reads the issue's live state, and searches for any PR already referencing the issue number — read-only, no issue-write calls. It prints a binary `STALE` (issue closed, or a referencing PR already exists) or `PROCEED` verdict and exits non-zero on `STALE`. A session runs it at both points ISSUE-106.md:14 names: immediately before starting execution, and immediately before the final push/spend. The sibling session-ops lesson (ISSUE-106.md:18, foreground waits on long detached child runs) ships as a usage note in the script's header comment, not a separate doc.

The claim protocol (ISSUE-106.md:15) is **rejected outright**, not deferred: its load-bearing premise — that a session has issue-write permission at dispatch time — is already falsified in the issue's own text ("dispatch instructions sometimes withhold" it). Adopting it now doesn't add a weaker signal, it adds a *misleading* one: no-comment would read as "not in progress" when it may only mean that session couldn't write. "Both" (ISSUE-106.md:16) is rejected for the same reason — it inherits claim's hole.

Full lock-based mechanization (a harness-enforced pre-spend hook blocking a genuinely concurrent dispatch) is **not attempted now**: this repo's forcing functions today (`adr-lint`, muda-review CI) gate at PR-review time, after spend already happened, and the harness starting `claude -p` sessions is external to this repo. Concurrent-instant collisions stay explicitly open.

## Justification
Prose-only is falsified by this repo's own history, not by inference: ADR 0042 shows a settled, superseded prose rule (fraction-met) recurring a third time with nothing mechanical to catch it. A script converts "remember to re-read the issue" into one command with a clear verdict — cheaper to comply with than a paragraph, so more likely followed under the pressure that skipped the never-existed rule in the original incident — but invocation stays voluntary, since no harness hook forces the call. Claim is rejected, not weakened-and-kept: a signal that silently degrades to noise for an unknown fraction of sessions is worse than no signal (false confidence), not merely weaker — the premortem's framing. A real lock is out of scope: it needs dispatch control we don't have, so building it now is gold-plate against an assumption (a controllable pre-spend hook point) that doesn't hold.

## Assumptions
- [verified] ADR 0025's fraction-met metric floored a third time despite being settled and superseded — a prose rule alone did not stop recurrence (docs/decisions/0042-experiment-methodology-and-prespend-discipline.md:13,22, read 2026-07-14).
- [verified] the claim protocol's stated premise (issue-write permission at dispatch) is already contradicted in the same issue that proposes it (ISSUE-106.md:15).
- [checkable] `scripts/check-issue-live.mjs` exists, contains no `gh issue comment`/`gh issue edit`/assignment calls (read-only), and exits non-zero on a closed issue or an existing referencing PR — owner: verifier at implementation.
- [unverifiable] a runnable script with a binary verdict is followed more reliably under dispatch pressure than the equivalent prose paragraph — REOPEN-IF: a duplicate-spend recurs with the script already present in the repo (i.e., it existed but was skipped).

## Rejected alternatives
- Prose-only sync-before-spend rule as literally drafted (ISSUE-106.md:14) — same enforcement class as ADR 0025, which recurred a third time (ADR 0042).
- Claim protocol / "Both" (ISSUE-106.md:15-16) — the permission premise is falsified in the issue's own text; ships false confidence, not a weaker safeguard.
- Harness-enforced pre-spend lock — not achievable now: this repo's gates (`adr-lint`, muda-review CI) fire at PR-review time, after spend; the dispatch harness is external and not something this repo controls.

## Revisit triggers
- A duplicate-spend recurs with the check script present in the repo → the script-over-prose assumption is false; escalate toward a harness-level hook, or accept the recurrence class as a standing cost.
- Dispatch instructions change to always grant issue-write permission → the claim protocol's rejected premise becomes viable; revisit adding it as a supplement to the sync check, not a replacement.
